title: Newport HT70 Plus Ventilator - Battery Not Charging or Shortened Runtime
description: Troubleshooting battery not charging, rapid drain, or short runtime issues on the Newport HT70 Plus ventilator.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the ventilator is in use, immediately transition the patient to a backup ventilator before troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Disconnect AC power and observe battery behavior to determine if the unit shuts off, runs briefly, or alarms.

steps.title: Check AC Power Source
steps.instructions: Reconnect AC power, verify outlet functionality, and confirm the ventilator powers on normally.

steps.title: Inspect Power Cord and Adapter
steps.instructions: Check the AC cord and power supply for damage and ensure all connections are secure.

steps.title: Confirm Charging Indicators
steps.instructions: Observe display indicators for charging status and absence of related alarms.

steps.title: Allow Adequate Charging Time
steps.instructions: Leave the ventilator connected to AC power long enough for the battery to recover and begin charging.

steps.title: Perform a Battery Runtime Check
steps.instructions: Disconnect AC power after charging and monitor runtime to verify battery capacity.

steps.title: Inspect Battery Compartment
steps.instructions: Check battery seating, contacts, and condition for corrosion, debris, or damage.

steps.title: Check for Environmental Factors
steps.instructions: Ensure the ventilator is used within proper temperature ranges to avoid battery performance issues.

CCR.Complaint: Ventilator battery not charging and unit shuts off when unplugged.
CCR.Cause: Battery failed to hold charge after full charging cycle.
CCR.Resolution: Removed ventilator from service and sent for battery replacement.

title: Newport HT70 Plus Ventilator - Circuit Leak or Disconnection Alarms
description: Troubleshooting circuit leak or disconnection alarms caused by tubing issues, loose connections, accessories, or setup problems affecting ventilation integrity.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Immediately assess the patient. If ventilation is compromised, switch to a backup ventilator or manual ventilation (BVM). Expected: Patient remains safely ventilated Why it matters: Circuit leaks can result in inadequate ventilation and patient harm

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm
steps.instructions: Observe the ventilator and confirm: “Circuit Leak” or “Disconnect” alarm present When the alarm occurs (startup, during breath delivery, intermittently) Expected: Alarm is reproducible or clearly observed Why it matters: Confirms a true system-detected issue vs transient event

steps.title: Check Patient Circuit Connections (Most Common Cause)
steps.instructions: Inspect the entire breathing circuit: Ensure circuit is fully connected from ventilator to patient interface Check wye, exhalation valve, and patient connection points Reseat all connections firmly Expected: Tight, secure connections throughout Why it matters: Loose fittings are the most frequent cause of leak/disconnect alarms

steps.title: Inspect for Visible Leaks or Damage
steps.instructions: Check tubing and components for: Cracks, holes, or tears Disconnected or partially seated tubing Damaged fittings or connectors If damage is found, replace the circuit. Expected: No visible leaks or compromised components Why it matters: Even small leaks can trigger alarms and reduce delivered volume

steps.title: Check Patient Interface (ET Tube / Mask / Tracheostomy)
steps.instructions: Ensure ET tube cuff is properly inflated (if applicable) Check mask seal if using non-invasive ventilation Verify trach connections are secure Expected: Proper seal at patient interface Why it matters: Patient-side leaks are common and often overlooked

steps.title: Inspect Exhalation Valve Assembly
steps.instructions: Ensure exhalation valve is properly installed and seated Check for contamination, moisture, or improper assembly Reseat or replace if needed Expected: Valve is clean, properly seated, and functioning Why it matters: Improper valve function can mimic circuit leaks

steps.title: Check Flow Sensor and Proximal Sensor Lines (If Equipped)
steps.instructions: Ensure sensor tubing is connected and not kinked Inspect for moisture buildup or blockage Replace or dry components if needed Expected: Accurate flow measurement without obstruction Why it matters: Faulty readings can trigger false leak alarms

steps.title: Run Pre-Use Check / Leak Test
steps.instructions: Perform the ventilator’s built-in circuit check or leak test. Expected: Leak test passes within acceptable limits Why it matters: Confirms system integrity outside of patient factors

steps.title: Swap with Known Good Circuit (Isolation Step)
steps.instructions: Replace the entire circuit with a known good setup. Expected: Alarm resolves with new circuit Why it matters: Quickly isolates circuit-related issues vs ventilator fault

CCR.Complaint: Ventilator alarming “circuit leak” during patient ventilation.
CCR.Cause: Loose connection found at exhalation valve assembly causing system leak.
CCR.Resolution: Reseated and secured connections; verified operation with leak test passed.

helpfulDetails: Patient interface type (ET tube, mask, trach)
Any moisture found in circuit or sensors
Final device status (returned to service or sent for repair)

title: Newport HT70 Plus Ventilator - Flow Sensor Calibration Errors
description: Troubleshooting flow sensor calibration failures, inaccurate readings, or alarms caused by setup issues, contamination, or environmental factors.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the ventilator from active patient use if calibration is required and transition the patient to a backup ventilator.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Run the flow sensor calibration and observe for failure, error messages, or inconsistent readings.

steps.title: Check Circuit and Patient Setup (Most Common Cause)
steps.instructions: Disconnect the circuit, ensure no kinks or occlusions, and remove inline accessories.

steps.title: Inspect Flow Sensor and Connections
steps.instructions: Check sensor seating, damage, and moisture; reseat securely.

steps.title: Check for Moisture or Contamination (Very Common)
steps.instructions: Inspect for condensation or debris, replace disposable parts, and dry reusable components.

steps.title: Ensure Proper Calibration Conditions
steps.instructions: Disconnect from patient, ensure proper setup, and eliminate environmental airflow interference.

steps.title: Power Cycle the Ventilator
steps.instructions: Turn off the unit, wait 30 seconds, power back on, and retry calibration.

steps.title: Verify Use of Correct Accessories
steps.instructions: Confirm only compatible circuits and sensors are being used.

steps.title: Attempt Calibration with Known Good Setup
steps.instructions: Swap in a known good sensor and circuit to isolate the issue.

CCR.Complaint: Ventilator displaying flow sensor calibration error and failing calibration.
CCR.Cause: Moisture found in flow sensor and circuit causing inaccurate readings.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced circuit and dried sensor; calibration completed successfully.

helpfulDetails: Calibration success or failure after steps

title: Newport HT70 Plus Ventilator - Pre-Use Check Failures
description: Troubleshooting pre-use check failures caused by circuit setup, leaks, sensors, or configuration issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not use the ventilator on a patient if it fails pre-use check. Move the patient to a verified working ventilator if needed.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Failure
steps.instructions: Run the pre-use check and observe the exact failure message or step and when it occurs during the sequence.

steps.title: Inspect Patient Circuit Setup (Most Common Cause)
steps.instructions: Check correct circuit type, ensure all tubing is connected, verify no missing components, and reseat all connections.

steps.title: Check for Circuit Leaks
steps.instructions: Inspect for loose fittings, cracked tubing, and poorly seated components. Replace with a known-good circuit if needed.

steps.title: Verify Flow Sensor Installation and Condition
steps.instructions: Confirm correct orientation, ensure it is clean and dry, fully seated, and swap with a known-good sensor if available.

steps.title: Check Exhalation Valve Function (if applicable)
steps.instructions: Inspect for proper placement, damage, obstruction, and ensure it is clean and functioning.

steps.title: Confirm Proper Test Setup (No Patient Connected)
steps.instructions: Ensure circuit is properly configured, no patient is connected, and a test lung is used if required.

steps.title: Check Internal Settings and Mode Configuration
steps.instructions: Verify correct circuit type, proper ventilation mode, and ensure no alarm limits interfere with testing.

steps.title: Power Cycle the Ventilator
steps.instructions: Turn the unit off, wait 10–15 seconds, power it back on, and rerun the pre-use check.

CCR.Complaint: Ventilator failed pre-use check and would not pass system test.
CCR.Cause: Leak identified in patient circuit due to loose connection at flow sensor.
CCR.Resolution: Reseated connections, replaced circuit, and confirmed unit passed pre-use check successfully.

helpfulDetails: Pre-use check failure step (leak test, calibration, pressure test)

title: Newport HT70 Plus Ventilator - Alarm Not Triggering or Misconfigured
description: Addresses missing, delayed, or incorrect alarm activation due to configuration, settings, or external setup issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions:
If alarms are not functioning properly, immediately move the patient to a backup ventilator.

Expected: Patient remains safely ventilated with active alarm protection
Why it matters: Alarm failure removes a critical safety layer for detecting patient distress

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions:
Power on the ventilator and observe alarm behavior.

Check:
- Are alarms not sounding at all?
- Are alarms delayed or inconsistent?
- Are visual alarms present but no audible alarm?

Expected: Issue is clearly reproducible
Why it matters: Confirms true alarm failure vs misunderstanding of alarm behavior

steps.title: Check Alarm Volume and Mute Status (Most Common Cause)
steps.instructions:
Inspect alarm audio settings:
- Ensure alarm volume is not set to minimum
- Confirm alarm silence/mute is not active
- Check for temporary silence timers

Expected: Audible alarms function at appropriate volume
Why it matters: Muted or minimized alarms are a frequent cause of “no alarm” complaints

steps.title: Review Alarm Limits and Settings
steps.instructions:
Check configured alarm thresholds:
- High/low pressure limits
- High/low tidal volume limits
- Apnea settings

Ensure limits are set appropriately for patient condition.

Expected: Alarm limits are within a realistic range to trigger alerts
Why it matters: Limits set too wide may prevent alarms from activating

steps.title: Trigger a Test Alarm Condition
steps.instructions:
Manually create a safe test condition (off patient):
- Disconnect circuit briefly to trigger disconnection alarm
- Simulate apnea if applicable

Expected: Alarm activates both visually and audibly
Why it matters: Confirms whether alarm system is functioning at all

steps.title: Check Alarm Indicator Functionality
steps.instructions:
Observe:
- Visual alarm indicators (lights, screen alerts)
- Audible alarm tones

If visual alarms work but no sound → likely speaker or audio issue
If neither works → possible system-level issue

Expected: Both visual and audible alarms function together
Why it matters: Helps isolate audio vs system failure

steps.title: Inspect External Accessories and Connections
steps.instructions:
Verify:
- Patient circuit properly connected
- Flow sensors and pressure lines correctly installed
- No bypassed or disconnected components

Expected: Proper connections allow accurate alarm triggering
Why it matters: Improper setup can prevent the ventilator from detecting fault conditions

steps.title: Check Power and System Status
steps.instructions:
Ensure stable power:
- Connected to AC or adequately charged battery
- No intermittent shutdowns or resets

Expected: Stable operation without power interruptions
Why it matters: Power instability can affect alarm system reliability

steps.title: Perform a System Restart
steps.instructions:
Power cycle the ventilator fully.

Expected: Alarm function returns to normal if issue was software-related
Why it matters: Clears temporary faults or configuration glitches

CCR.Complaint: Ventilator alarms not sounding during use.
CCR.Cause: Alarm volume found set to minimum and alarm limits set outside normal range.
CCR.Resolution: Adjusted alarm volume and reset alarm limits; verified proper alarm function with test condition.

helpfulDetails: Visual vs audible alarm behavior noted
Final device status (returned to service or sent for repair)
